open_gaps: The immutable statement's domain for n and k is not uniquely specified, so the discrete and semidiscrete interpretations give different answers.
Under the intended discrete interpretation, no proof or disproof of the universal assertion is supplied.
There is no global proof that S_2 intersect S_3 intersect S_4 intersect S_k is empty for every k at least 5.
There is no proof that two or more high-layer representations force total interior multiplicity at most three.
There is no construction producing exactly q interior representations for every q at least zero.
Neither the Pell branch nor the displacement-word branch provides arbitrary compatible multi-lifts.
No uniform method excludes unintended representations in an infinite constructed family.
No effective bound or complete decision procedure is supplied for the unbounded integer systems associated with scalable displacement words.
The finite computation stops at 10^30 and has no proved finite-to-infinite extension.
counterexample_searches: Checked a=70 under the two proposed domains: it has two discrete solutions and four semidiscrete solutions, confirming the stated domain dependence.
Checked the claimed exact multiplicities for 2, 6, 120, and 3003 by layer monotonicity and central lower bounds; these claims survive.
Checked the 3003 collision chain (78,2) to (15,5) to (14,6); its edge ratios are 1/21 and 1, consistent with the intended slope theorem.
Checked the first two adjacent Pell blocks: (15,5) to (14,6) and (104,39) to (103,40), including the recurrence, congruence, and admissibility conditions.
Re-executed the exact-integer enumeration through 10^30. The reported totals, repeated high-layer values, low-layer hits, and empty dangerous list were reproduced.
material_errors: The candidate explicitly reaches no terminal conclusion, so it fails the acceptance criterion requiring a proof or disproof of the exact original statement.

Claim 17 does not actually display its asserted polynomial equations: the entire left-hand side of each displacement identity is missing. As written, WORD_SYSTEM is not a defined system and cannot support later decision or enumeration claims.
The displayed definition of D_Z in Claim 1 is malformed. If read as requiring both coordinates to be at least two, it excludes the k=1 solutions used throughout Claims 1, 6, and 8.

The definitions of S_j and h(a) in Claim 10 are ill-typed as written: S_j is not properly displayed as a set of coefficient values, and h(a) is written as though integers k belonged to S_k rather than a belonging to S_k.

Claim 12 contains missing mathematical content in its displayed derivation and reciprocal-ratio chain. The intended averaging argument can be reconstructed and appears valid, but the written proof is not locally complete without that reconstruction.
Claim 15 contains a malformed equation where the required identity is p times a equals q times C(n-1,k-1). The subsequent divisibility argument relies on this repaired reading.
The finite certificate is valid only as a bounded computation and cannot discharge either global branch.
notes: Most elementary conditional claims survive after repairing several transcription defects: fixed-layer and half-row monotonicity, central growth, normalization, fiber ordering, low multiplicities, density for multiplicities one and two, the displacement identity, the intended slope comparison, the adjacent Pell family, Pell-block separation, the divisor invariant, and the reversible low-layer equations. The bounded enumeration was independently reproduced. These results form a useful conditional structural package, but they neither settle the domain ambiguity nor prove either the negative t=5 route or a positive exact-multiplicity construction. Therefore the candidate cannot pass.
